utterances: And out of that, he decided that there's a real need for this kind of service.
And he decided with some colleagues to found an organization of volunteers called Bedside Advocates that would offer this kind of help for people in need.
We don't practice medicine.
We're the advocates.
Some of us are physicians, some of us are nurses, others are laypeople.
The bottom line is we're accessible and try to relieve unnecessary anxiety and see that their needs are taken care of adequately, promptly.
I said, gee, that sounds really interesting.
I'd like to see how it works.
So Dr. Fine introduced me to one of his fellow volunteers.
He's a semi-retired physician in Boston.
I'm Doug Fiero.
I practice part-time.
I work at several community health centers here in Boston, and I also fill in for Boston Health Care for the Homeless.
Dr.
Fiero suggests that we meet at the apartment of one of his clients.
Her name is Betsaida Gutierrez.
She's 56 years old.
She has a lovely, warm, sunny disposition and a lot of medical problems.
We sit around her kitchen table and discuss her medical history.
Around 18 months ago, she had sharp stomach pains in her stomach.
She stopped eating and began to lose weight at a dramatic rate.
She was very ill at that point.
She was weak.
She could barely get up and walk.
Her weight went down from her usual 125 pounds all the way down to 77.
Dr. Fiero says it was getting pretty alarming.
The doctors didn't really have a good idea what was going on with you.
The reason they weren't direct in communicating is they didn't really know.
I didn't understand it at all.
I just had a sense that I was just wasting.
I was wasting, and there's nothing that can be done.
She didn't lack for doctors.
But the problem was the doctors weren't communicating very well with each other about what they were thinking and they weren't telling Gutierrez what they were thinking.
Eventually, the doctors decided that what she really needed was to have a good part of her colon removed.
And so the surgeons did that.
And they thought, well, that solves the problem.
But Dr. Fiero knew that they really hadn't.
We were quite sure there was more to this, because you were continuing to fail.
Finally, antibiotics and some nutritional counseling and support got her over the hump, and she began to gain weight and feel better.
Looking back on it now, Gutierrez says there's no way she could have done this on her own.
How big a problem is that?
How many people are denied coverage in terms of percentage for conditions they already have?

The number of people who can't get health insurance because of preexisting conditions is a very, very small number compared to the number of people who can't get health insurance because they can't afford it.
And that's the real issue, is affordability.

You see - and correct me if I'm wrong in characterizing your position, but you see this as so large a problem that the government can't pay for it and companies can't pay for it, so they have to come together.
Yeah, absolutely.
The United Auto Workers labor - working for the big three automakers: Chrysler, Ford and GM.
There is $90 billion of unfunded retiree health care cost sitting with those three companies.
It's got to the point where they may go broke.
They may go out of business.

And it's got to the point where the United Auto Workers, a labor union that had a lot to do with starting the tradition of health insurance in the employer environment, UAW is now at the table talking to the auto companies about changing that, because everyone understands that the employer can't by themselves afford this anymore, that we've got to change that relationship.
So, cost is absolutely the issue.
The government can't afford it by itself.

If Mrs. Clinton or any of the other candidates is successful in covering everyone in America, the next question they've got to ask is how are we going to be able to cover everyone in America the next year and the year after that and the year after that with cost going up at 6 to 10 percent while our economy is growing at 3 percent.
Wages are growing at 3 percent.
Health care costs just outstrip everything.
Bob Laszewski is president of Health Policy and Strategy Associates, based in Washington, D.C.
Thank you, sir.
Thank you.
As we mentioned, the New York City Board of Health is considering a plan to force restaurants there to severely limit their use of trans fats.
The board is also considering a proposal to make restaurants that serve burgers and fries and other standard fare list the calories in their products right on the menu.
Here's NPR's Margot Adler.

The hearing on trans fats was dominated by those who want to limit them, a veritable parade of doctors, nutritionists, school lunch officials, politicians - with a sprinkling of opposition expressed by restaurant trade associations and a couple of libertarians.
Until the 1990s, most people thought trans fats, partially hydrogenated vegetable oils, were healthier alternatives to butter.
They were also cheaper and restaurants switched.
Then scientific research showed that trans fats had a direct impact on heart disease.
New York City Council member Peter Vallone typified many speakers.
Trans fats kill kids.
Trans fats kill adults.
Ideally, the federal government should be doing this, but the food industry has so much influence there that Washington has not gotten up off its trans fat filled backsides to do anything.
And by the way, he said, those ads the other side is using saying ice cream is going to be taken away from little girls -
Some of the people who oppose this are telling big fat lies.
Fat was definitely the word of the day.
Speaking against the ban on trans fats and against listing calories on menus were a number of restaurant trade association officials.
Their main concern, a lack of supply of alternative oils that would hurt small businesses.
Sheila Cohn Weiss, who directs nutrition policy for the National Restaurant Association, said it would be years before crop supply was adequate.
Some restaurants will have no choice but to revert to higher saturated fat oils.
We don't believe the solution for New York City is to simply follow Denmark's lead, where food service companies have simply substituted palm oil for partially hydrogenated vegetable oil.
At least one doctor disputed that Denmark had increased the use of saturated fats.
Other opponents of the two plans said mandating a quick change would cause disaster in the New York economy.
After all, they said, many restaurant owners didn't even know what trans fats were.
And Audrey Silk, who campaigned long and hard against the smoking ban in the city, said I told you so.
Food is next on the slippery slope of government regulations in the nanny state.
We resent for public health a role and a power that it's never historically had and never was supposed to have.
Social engineering, eliminating choice and coercing behavior is not the American way.
But there were few voices echoing these sentiments.
Even some of the restaurant owners, and few were there to testify came out of their way, in some cases from California and Chicago to give support to the trans fat ban.
Winning the best fried chicken in Chicago contest didn't hurt.
Ina Pinknay of Ina's Restaurant in Chicago said she made the switch several years ago, and not only did it improve the fried chicken, it didn't hurt her pancake batter or her carrot cake.
A representative from Wendy's said his only objection was insisting that calories be listed only for big chains that already provide nutritional information.
Across the street, two demonstrations took place - one pro and one con.
The only advantage of the anti-trans fat crowd was they gave away free food.
Heather Umla(ph) and Ken Hoag(ph) pushed samples of potatoes and muffins toward me.
What are these?
What are these that we have here?
A trans fat free muffin that we made from Fancy Go Catering.
We're an organic catering company.
We support the use of non-trans fat oils.
And what are those over there?
Those are baby yellow potatoes and they're fried in the Whole Harvest No-Trans Oil, expeller pressed oil.
The New York City board of health is supposed to make its decision by December.
Margot Adler, NPR News.
New York.
And it's getting faster and cheaper almost by the minute.
One company is showing off one machine that looks like a fat thumb drive, plugs into a laptop, and supposedly spits out a sequence directly from a little blood within hours.
You could suck up a little sample and squirt it in there, kind of almost sci-fi, kind of, visions.
Already, it's gotten to the point where it can cost just $4,000 to sequence someone's genome.
So some doctors are starting to map cancer patients.
They're trying to find the mutation that's causing their tumor.
Oncologists can then sometimes find better drugs to treat them.
Other specialists are using sequencing to diagnose mysterious genetic conditions.
And some healthy people have even started getting mapped, just out of curiosity.
George Church is starting to recruit thousands of people around the world to get sequenced.
He's a prominent Harvard geneticist and a big advocate of widespread sequencing.
